prompt: Acting as an expert in K-12 education research and an experienced frontline teacher, research and analyze global case studies on the practical application of AIGC (AI-Generated Content) in primary and secondary school classrooms. Identify, categorize, and analyze various application approaches and their corresponding examples. The final report should present an overall framework, detailed category discussions, practical implementation methods, future trends, and recommendations for educators.
article:
## 1) What “AIGC” means in K–12 practice (2023–2026) and where its capability boundaries are

### 1.1 Working definition for schools
Across current technical and education-policy sources, **AIGC / generative AI** is best treated as **AI that produces new content** (not just predictions) from prompts—**text, images, audio, video, code**, and increasingly multimodal outputs—typically via large “foundation models” trained on very large datasets [[3]](https://consult.education.gov.uk/digital-strategy/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/), [[4]](https://www.oecd.org/en/publications/oecd-digital-education-outlook-2023_c74f03de-en/full-report/emerging-governance-of-generative-ai-in-education_3cbd6269.html). Technical surveys explicitly define AIGC as AI-generated content across modalities (text/image/video/3D/audio) using modern ML/DL approaches, and distinguish **single-modality** generation from **cross-modality** generation (e.g., text-to-image) [[1]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2308.14177.pdf), [[2]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2308.14177).

### 1.2 Practical subtype taxonomy educators can actually use
A school-useful taxonomy combines **modality** and **interaction pattern**:

**(A) By modality/output**
- **Text / code** generation (chatbots, drafting, summarizing, question-writing) [[3]](https://consult.education.gov.uk/digital-strategy/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/), [[47]](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/generative-artificial-intelligence-ai-in-education)  
- **Image** generation (illustrations, concept art, visual explanations) [[3]](https://consult.education.gov.uk/digital-strategy/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/), [[1]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2308.14177.pdf)  
- **Audio** generation (voice, music, narration) [[3]](https://consult.education.gov.uk/digital-strategy/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/), [[2]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2308.14177)  
- **Video** generation (emerging classroom uses; higher risk and higher compute; quality varies) [[3]](https://consult.education.gov.uk/digital-strategy/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/), [[2]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2308.14177)  
- **3D** generation (still niche for K–12; more common in specialized STEM/CTE contexts) [[2]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2308.14177)

**(B) By interaction pattern**
- **Chat / assistant** (iterative prompting; tutoring-like dialogue) [[3]](https://consult.education.gov.uk/digital-strategy/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/), [[4]](https://www.oecd.org/en/publications/oecd-digital-education-outlook-2023_c74f03de-en/full-report/emerging-governance-of-generative-ai-in-education_3cbd6269.html)  
- **Rewrite / transform** (translate, simplify, adjust reading level, correct grammar/tone) [[106]](https://www.eursc.eu/BasicTexts/2025-01-D-65-en-2.pdf), [[47]](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/generative-artificial-intelligence-ai-in-education)  
- **Generate artifacts** (worksheets, rubrics, quizzes, exemplars, slides) [[33]](https://educationendowmentfoundation.org.uk/projects-and-evaluation/projects/choices-in-edtech-using-generative-ai-chatgpt-for-ks3-science-lesson-preparation-2024-teacher-choices-trial), [[70]](https://computingeducationresearch.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/AICT-Guidance.pdf)  
- **Agentic/automated action** (tools that can “do tasks” autonomously—scheduling, workflows). Education guidance increasingly flags this as distinct and requiring stronger governance [[6]](https://www.teachai.org/toolkit).

### 1.3 Capability boundaries that matter in classrooms
Even when outputs look fluent, schools repeatedly encounter consistent boundaries:

- **Hallucinations / confabulations**: outputs may be plausible but false; DfE explicitly warns of “hallucination” risk and requires professional judgment and checking [[47]](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/generative-artificial-intelligence-ai-in-education); NZ similarly warns AI can produce plausible but incorrect detail and requires review [[52]](https://www.education.govt.nz/school/digital-technology/generative-ai). NIST treats “confabulation” as a key GenAI risk category [[56]](https://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/ai/NIST.AI.600-1.pdf).  
- **Bias + cultural/linguistic gaps**: output reflects training-data skews; NZ notes models often reflect dominant languages/cultures and may be weak on indigenous knowledge and certain languages [[52]](https://www.education.govt.nz/school/digital-technology/generative-ai).  
- **“Jagged frontier” performance** (excellent at some tasks, unpredictable failures): explicitly noted in the European Schools framework [[106]](https://www.eursc.eu/BasicTexts/2025-01-D-65-en-2.pdf).  
- **Age/safeguarding constraints + harmful content risks**: DfE and other systems emphasize filtering/monitoring and close supervision for pupil-facing use [[47]](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/generative-artificial-intelligence-ai-in-education), with product-level safety expectations (filtering, logging, jailbreak resistance) increasingly formalized [[107]](https://dera.ioe.ac.uk/id/eprint/41041/1/Generative%20AI%20product%20safety%20expectations%20-%20GOV.UK.pdf).  
- **Privacy and data protection limits**: many authorities advise **not putting personal data into public GenAI tools** [[47]](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/generative-artificial-intelligence-ai-in-education), [[52]](https://www.education.govt.nz/school/digital-technology/generative-ai); privacy vetting highlights that prompts/outputs can become education records and trigger legal obligations (FERPA/PPRA/COPPA contexts) [[54]](https://fpf.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/Ed_AI_legal_compliance.pdf_FInal_OCT24.pdf).  
- **Academic integrity disruption**: OECD notes early debates centered on “cheating” and assessment disruption for homework/assignments/exams [[4]](https://www.oecd.org/en/publications/oecd-digital-education-outlook-2023_c74f03de-en/full-report/emerging-governance-of-generative-ai-in-education_3cbd6269.html).  
- **Tool dependence / vendor and procurement risk**: governance toolkits emphasize contracts, transparency, and lifecycle monitoring rather than ad hoc teacher-by-teacher adoption [[53]](https://www.cosn.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/Cosn_CGCS-K-12-Gen-AI-Maturity-Tool-Conference.pdf), [[56]](https://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/ai/NIST.AI.600-1.pdf).

---

## 2) A classification framework for AIGC classroom use (a practical “map”)

A robust way to categorize global classroom approaches is a **three-layer framework**: **Purpose × User × Depth of change**, with **Risk tiering** overlay.

### 2.1 Layer 1 — “Who is using it?”
Education governance frameworks converge on:
- **Teacher-facing** use (planning, resource creation, feedback drafts, admin efficiency) [[47]](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/generative-artificial-intelligence-ai-in-education), [[33]](https://educationendowmentfoundation.org.uk/projects-and-evaluation/projects/choices-in-edtech-using-generative-ai-chatgpt-for-ks3-science-lesson-preparation-2024-teacher-choices-trial), [[70]](https://computingeducationresearch.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/AICT-Guidance.pdf)  
- **Pupil-facing** use (tutoring, writing support, inquiry partner, creation tools) [[106]](https://www.eursc.eu/BasicTexts/2025-01-D-65-en-2.pdf), [[28]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2024.1288723/full), [[30]](https://repository.isls.org/bitstream/1/10943/1/ICLS2024_2217-2218.pdf)  
- **System-facing** use (district/state tools, procurement standards, monitoring, student data governance) [[53]](https://www.cosn.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/Cosn_CGCS-K-12-Gen-AI-Maturity-Tool-Conference.pdf), [[20]](https://files.eric.ed.gov/fulltext/ED661924.pdf), [[58]](https://scottybreaksitdown.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/Australian-Framework-for-Generative-AI-in-Schools.pdf)

### 2.2 Layer 2 — “What is it doing?”
Use five functional buckets that align well with real classroom routines:
1) **Create** (new text/media/code) [[2]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2308.14177), [[70]](https://computingeducationresearch.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/AICT-Guidance.pdf)  
2) **Transform** (translate, simplify, re-level, style/tone) [[106]](https://www.eursc.eu/BasicTexts/2025-01-D-65-en-2.pdf), [[70]](https://computingeducationresearch.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/AICT-Guidance.pdf)  
3) **Explain/Coach** (tutoring dialogue, step-by-step scaffolds) [[30]](https://repository.isls.org/bitstream/1/10943/1/ICLS2024_2217-2218.pdf), [[37]](https://michiganvirtual.org/blog/have-you-considered-ai-in-your-classroom-a-khanmigo-pilot-story/)  
4) **Assess/Feedback (assistive, not autonomous)** (draft feedback, rubric suggestions, question generation) [[63]](https://www.sreb.org/sites/main/files/file-attachments/2025_ai_in_k-12classroom_guidance.pdf), [[70]](https://computingeducationresearch.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/AICT-Guidance.pdf)  
5) **Manage/Operate** (emails, reports, planning docs; agentic workflows are emerging) [[63]](https://www.sreb.org/sites/main/files/file-attachments/2025_ai_in_k-12classroom_guidance.pdf), [[6]](https://www.teachai.org/toolkit)

### 2.3 Layer 3 — “How much does it change instruction?” (SAMR + TPACK + UDL)
- **SAMR**: Substitution → Augmentation → Modification → Redefinition [[11]](https://www.hippasus.com/rrpweblog/archives/2014/06/29/LearningTechnologySAMRModel.pdf).  
  - Many early school uses are **Substitution/Augmentation** (draft a quiz, rewrite a worksheet).  
  - The transformative ceiling is **Modification/Redefinition** (new multimodal projects, iterative design with critique cycles, classroom simulations, personalized pathways).  
- **TPACK**: effective AIGC use depends on the intersection of **content + pedagogy + technology in context**; context-blind “generic prompts” are a common failure mode [[12]](http://www.matt-koehler.com/publications/Koehler_et_al_2013.pdf), [[13]](https://one2oneheights.pbworks.com/f/MISHRA_PUNYA.pdf).  
- **UDL**: AIGC is especially powerful for **multiple means of representation/action-expression/engagement**—e.g., simplified text, translations, alternative formats, scaffolds for writing and expression [[14]](http://udloncampus.cast.org/wicket/resource/org.cast.cwm.xml.FileXmlDocumentSource/usr/local/tomcat/content/downloads/udlg_graphicorganizer_v2-2_numbers-no.pdf), [[58]](https://scottybreaksitdown.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/Australian-Framework-for-Generative-AI-in-Schools.pdf).

### 2.4 Risk tier overlay (what needs the strongest guardrails)
A simple school-ready tiering (supported by multiple guidance sources) is:

- **Tier 1 (Lower risk): Teacher drafting + generic materials** (no student personal data) — encouraged by DfE as having “more immediate benefits and fewer risks” [[47]](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/generative-artificial-intelligence-ai-in-education).  
- **Tier 2 (Medium): Student learning supports** (tutoring, writing coaching, translation) — requires close supervision, explicit instruction, and safe tools [[47]](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/generative-artificial-intelligence-ai-in-education), [[58]](https://scottybreaksitdown.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/Australian-Framework-for-Generative-AI-in-Schools.pdf).  
- **Tier 3 (High): Decisions affecting student rights/opportunity** (grading, placement, admissions, IEP generation, proctoring) — triggers the strongest privacy/accuracy/bias governance; EU AI Act flags several education uses (e.g., exam scoring) as high-risk and bans some practices (e.g., emotion recognition in education) [[105]](https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/policies/regulatory-framework-ai), [[57]](https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/annex/3/). Privacy vetting warns against relying on GenAI outputs for substantive educational decisions due to hallucinations/record integrity and terms-of-service constraints [[54]](https://fpf.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/Ed_AI_legal_compliance.pdf_FInal_OCT24.pdf).

---

## 3) Global case-study set: what has been tried, where, and under what conditions

Below are **well-documented (or at least clearly described) implementations** spanning multiple regions, with the key contextual variables needed to compare them.

### 3.1 Teacher-facing productivity and planning (strongest causal evidence so far)
**England (KS3 Science) — EEF/NFER cluster RCT of ChatGPT for lesson/resource prep**  
- **What**: Teachers used ChatGPT for lesson and resource preparation with a support guide; comparison group did not use GenAI for those lessons [[33]](https://educationendowmentfoundation.org.uk/projects-and-evaluation/projects/choices-in-edtech-using-generative-ai-chatgpt-for-ks3-science-lesson-preparation-2024-teacher-choices-trial), [[34]](https://d2tic4wvo1iusb.cloudfront.net/production/documents/projects/chatgpt_in_lesson_planning_-_evaluation_report.pdf).  
- **Scale**: 68 schools, 259 teachers; randomization at school level [[33]](https://educationendowmentfoundation.org.uk/projects-and-evaluation/projects/choices-in-edtech-using-generative-ai-chatgpt-for-ks3-science-lesson-preparation-2024-teacher-choices-trial), [[34]](https://d2tic4wvo1iusb.cloudfront.net/production/documents/projects/chatgpt_in_lesson_planning_-_evaluation_report.pdf).  
- **Outcomes**: Average lesson/resource prep time fell from **81.5 to 56.2 minutes/week** (saving **25.3 minutes/week**, ~31% reduction). **No evidence quality decreased** via blinded expert review [[33]](https://educationendowmentfoundation.org.uk/projects-and-evaluation/projects/choices-in-edtech-using-generative-ai-chatgpt-for-ks3-science-lesson-preparation-2024-teacher-choices-trial), [[34]](https://d2tic4wvo1iusb.cloudfront.net/production/documents/projects/chatgpt_in_lesson_planning_-_evaluation_report.pdf).  
- **How it was used**: “Light use”—often for one or two activities (questions/quizzes, ideas) rather than entire lessons [[33]](https://educationendowmentfoundation.org.uk/projects-and-evaluation/projects/choices-in-edtech-using-generative-ai-chatgpt-for-ks3-science-lesson-preparation-2024-teacher-choices-trial), [[34]](https://d2tic4wvo1iusb.cloudfront.net/production/documents/projects/chatgpt_in_lesson_planning_-_evaluation_report.pdf).  
- **Key contextual variables**: subject = science; structured implementation (learning period + guide); workload metric via diaries; strong evaluation design [[34]](https://d2tic4wvo1iusb.cloudfront.net/production/documents/projects/chatgpt_in_lesson_planning_-_evaluation_report.pdf).

**UK primary (in progress) — Oak National Academy “Aila” RCT (results expected 2026)**  
- **What**: AI lesson-planning assistant aimed at saving time and tailoring materials (e.g., literacy levels, local examples) [[35]](https://educationendowmentfoundation.org.uk/news/new-trial-ai-powered-lesson-planning).  
- **Scale**: recruiting ~450 KS2 teachers from 86 primary schools; independent evaluation; outcomes include time spent planning and blinded resource-quality review [[35]](https://educationendowmentfoundation.org.uk/news/new-trial-ai-powered-lesson-planning).  
- **Note**: evidence pending; included as an indicator of where rigorous evaluation is headed [[35]](https://educationendowmentfoundation.org.uk/news/new-trial-ai-powered-lesson-planning).

**Australia (NSW) — “NSWEduChat” purpose-built GenAI tool trial**
- **What**: Department-built, cloud-hosted tool in Sydney with content filtering, topic restrictions, moderation; designed to encourage reasoning rather than direct answers; aligned to NSW AI Assessment Framework [[31]](https://www.digital.nsw.gov.au/strategy/case-studies/nsweduchat).  
- **Scale**: Started Term 1 2024 in 16 schools → 50 schools Term 2; staff + Stage 3–6 students; expanded to all staff in Terms 3–4; continued into 2025 [[31]](https://www.digital.nsw.gov.au/strategy/case-studies/nsweduchat).  
- **Evidence**: Reported “teacher work efficiencies” and high engagement; no published student outcome metrics in the cited report [[31]](https://www.digital.nsw.gov.au/strategy/case-studies/nsweduchat).  
- **Context variables**: state-run infrastructure, integrated safeguards, professional learning + technical support [[31]](https://www.digital.nsw.gov.au/strategy/case-studies/nsweduchat).

**India (Karnataka) — “Shiksha copilot” human-in-the-loop teacher lesson planning system**
- **What**: LLM + retrieval (RAG) generates drafts; **human curators** review for quality/accuracy/context; teachers receive curated lesson plans (English/Kannada) and adapt [[92]](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.00456v1).  
- **Scale**: 1,043 teachers, 23 curators; deployed Dec 2024–Mar 2025; logs + interviews + surveys [[92]](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.00456v1).  
- **Outcomes**: Reduced lesson planning time and teacher stress (reported as “measurable reductions” though the excerpt does not provide effect sizes); Kannada translations required significant edits; tool used for both instruction and bureaucratic documentation; systemic constraints limited deeper pedagogy change [[92]](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.00456v1).  
- **Why it matters**: A concrete “Global South” model showing **curation as a safety/quality layer** rather than pure self-serve chat [[92]](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.00456v1).

### 3.2 Student-facing personalization and tutoring (emerging evidence; outcomes vary with scaffolding)
**Uruguay — real-time personalized lesson materials (Grades 4–6)**
- **What**: Classroom test lessons using **ChatGPT-3.5/4** + **Midjourney** to generate and *dynamically adjust* text, illustrations, and exercises to proficiency groups (basic/medium/advanced) [[28]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2024.1288723/full).  
- **Scale/setting**: 110 students, grades 4–6 across two schools; students on laptops; assessment via Digileac framework [[28]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2024.1288723/full).  
- **Outcomes**: Authors report improved motivation and performance and meeting curriculum learning objectives; also documents risks (privacy, divides, accuracy) [[28]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2024.1288723/full).  
- **Key insight**: A concrete example of **AIGC-enabled differentiation at the point of instruction**, not just homework support [[28]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2024.1288723/full).  
- **Caveat**: As reported, still early-stage; interpret results with attention to design and local context [[28]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2024.1288723/full).

**Singapore — GenAI programming chatbot (“MyBotBuddy”)**
- **What**: GenAI chatbot scaffolded with step-by-step instructions and prompt engineering; supports debugging, explaining lines, generating/simplifying code [[30]](https://repository.isls.org/bitstream/1/10943/1/ICLS2024_2217-2218.pdf).  
- **Design**: Half-day in-class quasi-experimental program (pre-test → intervention using chatbot → post-test + survey). Consent included parents and ministry [[30]](https://repository.isls.org/bitstream/1/10943/1/ICLS2024_2217-2218.pdf).  
- **Findings**: Two contrasting student cases show that outcomes depend heavily on interaction patterns—iterative clarification and engagement helped one student; shortcutting behaviors limited benefits for another [[30]](https://repository.isls.org/bitstream/1/10943/1/ICLS2024_2217-2218.pdf).  
- **Key implication**: **Student self-regulation** and “how students talk to the bot” is as important as model capability [[30]](https://repository.isls.org/bitstream/1/10943/1/ICLS2024_2217-2218.pdf).

**United States (Michigan) — multi-district Khanmigo pilots**
- **What**: Two pilots exploring Khan Academy’s AI tutor/assistant (Khanmigo) in classrooms; included PD sessions and surveys [[37]](https://michiganvirtual.org/blog/have-you-considered-ai-in-your-classroom-a-khanmigo-pilot-story/).  
- **Scale**: Pilot 1 (Spring 2024) 19 teachers/687 students/14 districts focused on Algebra I; Pilot 2 (Fall 2024–Spring 2025) 24 teachers/1102 students/8 districts open across subjects/grades, more emphasis on teacher tools [[37]](https://michiganvirtual.org/blog/have-you-considered-ai-in-your-classroom-a-khanmigo-pilot-story/).  
- **Outcomes**: Increases in teacher confidence; student use increased from monthly to weekly (reported); mixed student behavior (productive vs workaround); need explicit modeling of good use and expectations [[37]](https://michiganvirtual.org/blog/have-you-considered-ai-in-your-classroom-a-khanmigo-pilot-story/).  
- **Evidence type**: implementation study (perceptions + usage), not causal achievement evaluation [[37]](https://michiganvirtual.org/blog/have-you-considered-ai-in-your-classroom-a-khanmigo-pilot-story/).

**Khan Academy platform learning evidence (non-GenAI-specific but relevant to “tutoring ecosystems”)**
- Large-scale evidence associates 30+ minutes/week usage with greater-than-expected learning gains on MAP Growth (grades 3–8, 2022–23) [[74]](https://blog.khanacademy.org/khan-academy-efficacy-results-november-2024/).  
- Khan Academy notes Khanmigo impact studies are underway (not yet in the cited efficacy report) [[74]](https://blog.khanacademy.org/khan-academy-efficacy-results-november-2024/).  
- Practical implication: For “AI tutor” initiatives, **dosage and practice structure** matter; the GenAI layer must integrate into a coherent practice routine [[74]](https://blog.khanacademy.org/khan-academy-efficacy-results-november-2024/).

### 3.3 National / system-level “scale up” initiatives (important for governance patterns)
**Estonia — AI Leap 2025 (national secondary system integration)**
- **What**: National initiative (President-led, public-private) to provide AI tools and teacher training; OpenAI describes providing **ChatGPT Edu** + APIs and support, including custom GPTs for teaching and time-saving admin tasks [[80]](https://e-estonia.com/ai-leap-2025-estonia-sets-ai-standard-in-education/), [[81]](https://openai.com/index/estonia-schools-and-chatgpt/).  
- **Scale**: Phase 1 (Sept 2025) aims at 20,000 grade 10–11 students + 3,000 teachers; expansion in 2026 includes vocational and additional cohorts [[80]](https://e-estonia.com/ai-leap-2025-estonia-sets-ai-standard-in-education/).  
- **Governance signals**: Reporting indicates agreements on data management to meet European privacy rules, including not training models on student data and controlling data storage locations [[83]](https://theinnovator.news/estonias-ai-leap/).  
- **Why it matters**: A leading example of **national procurement + privacy contracting + teacher training at scale** [[80]](https://e-estonia.com/ai-leap-2025-estonia-sets-ai-standard-in-education/), [[83]](https://theinnovator.news/estonias-ai-leap/).

**China — Ministry of Education “AI education bases”**
- **What**: 184 primary/secondary schools designated as AI education bases to promote AI education, teacher training, curriculum development, and resource sharing [[89]](https://english.www.gov.cn/news/202402/23/content_WS65d85f47c6d0868f4e8e44a7.html), [[90]](http://education.chinadaily.com.cn/2024-02/23/c_965896.htm).  
- **Evidence**: System initiative; outcome metrics not in the cited releases [[89]](https://english.www.gov.cn/news/202402/23/content_WS65d85f47c6d0868f4e8e44a7.html), [[90]](http://education.chinadaily.com.cn/2024-02/23/c_965896.htm).

**South Korea — AI digital textbook approvals (2025 use)**
- **What**: Approved 76 AI digital textbooks; used in specific grades and subjects (English, math, coding) [[87]](https://english.moe.go.kr/boardCnts/viewRenewal.do).  
- **Teacher concerns**: Survey reporting high concern about student dependency and plagiarism; fewer than half of teachers reported teaching students how to use GenAI [[88]](https://www.koreaherald.com/article/10652914).  
- **Implication**: Scaling tools without student-use pedagogy and teacher training creates predictable implementation gaps [[88]](https://www.koreaherald.com/article/10652914).

### 3.4 Classroom “infusion” examples (qualitative but instructive)
**Finland — upper-secondary algebra course (15 weeks)**
- **What studied**: How students actually prompt and what attitudes they show while using ChatGPT in math problem solving [[84]](https://journals.helsinki.fi/lumat/article/download/2877/2306/11219).  
- **Findings**: Students found it easy/helpful, but spontaneous use was limited and prompts showed little goal-setting/reflection; suggests without scaffolding students default to quick answers [[84]](https://journals.helsinki.fi/lumat/article/download/2877/2306/11219).  
- **Implication**: You need **explicit self-regulated learning scaffolds** (goal → attempt → prompt → verify → reflect), not just access [[84]](https://journals.helsinki.fi/lumat/article/download/2877/2306/11219).

**Japan — senior high school project work**
- Students used GenAI to generate candidate titles for a video news report; also learned how training data volume affects recognition accuracy (rock-paper-scissors training activity) and discussed data misuse risks [[86]](https://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/news/backstories/3898/).  
- Teachers and experts emphasized the need for teacher training and information security/media literacy guidance [[86]](https://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/news/backstories/3898/).

**Shanghai (China) — grade 5 poetry-style generation in group project**
- Students used AI to generate poetry in the style of an 11th-century poet; teacher could view prompts and responses; teacher facilitated discussion about what AI was doing [[91]](https://www.k12dive.com/news/what-can-us-schools-learn-about-ai-education-from-their-chinese-counterpa/809618/).  
- Value: a concrete model of **teacher-visible prompting + classroom discussion** rather than hidden AI use [[91]](https://www.k12dive.com/news/what-can-us-schools-learn-about-ai-education-from-their-chinese-counterpa/809618/).

**Kenya (Taita-Taveta County) — secondary schools survey study**
- Reported early AI influence on lesson prep and personalization plus automation of routine tasks; major constraints include infrastructure, connectivity, costs, and limited training [[95]](https://rsisinternational.org/journals/ijriss/uploads/vol9-iss12-pg1292-1299-202601_pdf.pdf).  
- Value: highlights “implementation basics” (devices, bandwidth, PD) as binding constraints in many contexts [[95]](https://rsisinternational.org/journals/ijriss/uploads/vol9-iss12-pg1292-1299-202601_pdf.pdf).

---

## 4) Application categories and “how it’s done”: concrete classroom approaches, examples, and implementation methods

Below is a **category map** that aligns with observed global use and the strongest guidance. For each: (a) what it looks like, (b) representative examples, (c) practical methods teachers use, (d) what to watch for.

### Category 1 — Teacher lesson planning & material generation (Teacher-facing, Tier 1)
**What it looks like**
- Drafting lesson structures, quizzes, exemplars, slides, differentiation versions, substitute plans; then teacher edits and aligns to curriculum [[33]](https://educationendowmentfoundation.org.uk/projects-and-evaluation/projects/choices-in-edtech-using-generative-ai-chatgpt-for-ks3-science-lesson-preparation-2024-teacher-choices-trial), [[70]](https://computingeducationresearch.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/AICT-Guidance.pdf).

**Representative examples**
- England RCT: workload reduction with unchanged resource quality, even with “light use” focused on quizzes/questions and activity ideas [[33]](https://educationendowmentfoundation.org.uk/projects-and-evaluation/projects/choices-in-edtech-using-generative-ai-chatgpt-for-ks3-science-lesson-preparation-2024-teacher-choices-trial), [[34]](https://d2tic4wvo1iusb.cloudfront.net/production/documents/projects/chatgpt_in_lesson_planning_-_evaluation_report.pdf).  
- NSW EduChat: government-built tool with moderation and professional learning for staff [[31]](https://www.digital.nsw.gov.au/strategy/case-studies/nsweduchat).  
- India Shiksha copilot: curated lesson plans, bilingual outputs, strong “human in the loop” via curators [[92]](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.00456v1).

**How-to methods that work in practice**
1) **Use AI as both “input partner” and “output generator”** (not output-only). A case study of backward planning found teachers who sought both AI input (ideas/refinement) and output (worksheets/quizzes) reported productivity/work-quality gains, unlike output-only users [[68]](https://scale.stanford.edu/ai/repository/backwards-planning-generative-ai-case-study-evidence-us-k12-teachers).  
2) **Attach or paste your real plan, then ask for a constrained transformation.** For substitute plans, a practical pattern is: provide the lesson plan and request a sub-ready version with explicit components and time stamps (bellringer, Think-Pair-Share directions, exit ticket, transitions) [[71]](https://www.edutopia.org/article/ai-created-sub-plans-right-prompts-mean-better-outputs/).  
3) **Build a “teacher verification routine.”** Guidance stresses that non-fiction materials must be fact-checked; math outputs can be especially error-prone; teacher remains responsible [[70]](https://computingeducationresearch.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/AICT-Guidance.pdf), [[47]](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/generative-artificial-intelligence-ai-in-education).  

**What to watch for**
- Hallucinated facts, invented sources, misalignment to local standards [[47]](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/generative-artificial-intelligence-ai-in-education), [[65]](https://www.oregon.gov/ode/educator-resources/teachingcontent/Documents/ODE_Generative_Artificial_Intelligence_(AI)_in_K-12_Classrooms_2023.pdf).  
- Copyright/IP uncertainty in generated materials; many frameworks require copyright compliance and avoiding using protected materials for model training without permission [[58]](https://scottybreaksitdown.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/Australian-Framework-for-Generative-AI-in-Schools.pdf), [[47]](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/generative-artificial-intelligence-ai-in-education).  
- Tool sprawl: if every teacher uses different tools on personal accounts, privacy and equity risks rise (also observed in UK teacher survey patterns) [[39]](https://www.turing.ac.uk/sites/default/files/2025-06/understanding_the_impacts_of_generative_ai_use_on_children_-_wp1_report.pdf).

---

### Category 2 — Differentiation & personalized content (Teacher-facing + pupil-facing, Tier 2)
**What it looks like**
- Multiple reading levels; alternative explanations; interest-based examples; scaffolded practice sets; language supports.

**Representative examples**
- Uruguay: real-time generation and adjustment of lesson text/illustrations/exercises by proficiency groups in grades 4–6 [[28]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2024.1288723/full).  
- NSW EduChat: designed to guide reasoning rather than provide direct answers; used across Stage 3–6 [[31]](https://www.digital.nsw.gov.au/strategy/case-studies/nsweduchat).

**How-to implementation methods**
1) **Differentiate by “input constraints,” not just “simplify.”** Ask for: (a) key vocabulary pre-teach list, (b) short version, (c) standard, (d) extension tasks—then check for rigor drift.  
2) **Use UDL as the planning lens.** Generate multiple representations (visual explanation, short summary, glossary) and multiple means of expression (sentence frames, alternate formats) aligned to UDL principles [[14]](http://udloncampus.cast.org/wicket/resource/org.cast.cwm.xml.FileXmlDocumentSource/usr/local/tomcat/content/downloads/udlg_graphicorganizer_v2-2_numbers-no.pdf).  
3) **Prefer controlled/curated pipelines for high-scale differentiation.** The Karnataka model shows a scalable method: LLM drafts + retrieval + curator review + teacher adaptation [[92]](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.00456v1).

**Watch for**
- Equity: unequal access can widen gaps; ODE notes GenAI can be especially beneficial for multilingual learners and students with disabilities—so bans can also create inequity, but unstructured access can worsen inequity too [[65]](https://www.oregon.gov/ode/educator-resources/teachingcontent/Documents/ODE_Generative_Artificial_Intelligence_(AI)_in_K-12_Classrooms_2023.pdf).  
- Bias and cultural mismatch (especially for local histories/indigenous knowledge) [[52]](https://www.education.govt.nz/school/digital-technology/generative-ai).

---

### Category 3 — Tutoring, coaching, and step-by-step support (Tier 2; highly sensitive to pedagogy)
**What it looks like**
- AI tutor that asks questions, provides hints, checks reasoning, supports practice routines.

**Representative examples**
- Singapore MyBotBuddy coding tutor: outcomes depended on student interaction habits [[30]](https://repository.isls.org/bitstream/1/10943/1/ICLS2024_2217-2218.pdf).  
- Michigan Khanmigo pilots: mixed student behavior; strong need for modeling and expectations [[37]](https://michiganvirtual.org/blog/have-you-considered-ai-in-your-classroom-a-khanmigo-pilot-story/).

**How-to methods**
1) **Teach a “tutor protocol” explicitly.** Finland’s math case study shows students rarely use goal-setting/reflection prompts spontaneously; teachers must teach the protocol (goal → attempt → ask for hint → verify → reflect) [[84]](https://journals.helsinki.fi/lumat/article/download/2877/2306/11219).  
2) **Require evidence of thinking.** Have students submit: first attempt, AI conversation snippet, what they accepted/rejected and why, and a final explanation in their own words. This reduces shortcutting and supports academic integrity (also aligns with process-based assessment redesign guidance) [[65]](https://www.oregon.gov/ode/educator-resources/teachingcontent/Documents/ODE_Generative_Artificial_Intelligence_(AI)_in_K-12_Classrooms_2023.pdf), [[73]](https://www.edutopia.org/article/student-use-ai-helpful-framework/).  
3) **Use “traffic light permissions” for AI** (green/yellow/red tasks), plus iterative routines (PROMPT/EDIT) and transparency/annotation requirements [[73]](https://www.edutopia.org/article/student-use-ai-helpful-framework/).  

**Watch for**
- Overreliance and “dependency” concerns are widely reported by teachers (e.g., Korea survey) [[88]](https://www.koreaherald.com/article/10652914).  
- Accuracy: tutoring outputs must be verified; teachers should avoid positioning the AI as authoritative [[47]](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/generative-artificial-intelligence-ai-in-education), [[52]](https://www.education.govt.nz/school/digital-technology/generative-ai).

---

### Category 4 — Feedback, assessment design, and grading assistance (Teacher-facing Tier 2–3 depending on stakes)
**What it looks like**
- Draft rubric-based feedback; generate question banks; create exemplars; “first-pass” marking support—**but not autonomous grading**.

**Examples**
- NYC teachers using AI tools for first-pass grading and saving time, while checking and adjusting scores [[38]](https://www.chalkbeat.org/newyork/2024/10/18/nyc-teachers-experiment-with-artificial-intelligence-powered-tools/).  
- Multiple frameworks explicitly warn against AI as sole grader; European Schools framework states teachers should not rely on GenAI tools to assess learning [[106]](https://www.eursc.eu/BasicTexts/2025-01-D-65-en-2.pdf).

**How-to methods**
1) **Use GenAI to generate *feedback language options*, not final grades.** Draft comments aligned to your rubric; teacher finalizes.  
2) **Use GenAI to create “anchor papers” and then have students practice marking.** AICT guidance explicitly suggests generating answers to essay-type questions and asking students to mark them [[70]](https://computingeducationresearch.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/AICT-Guidance.pdf).  
3) **Design assessment tasks that specify AI permissions.** Multiple frameworks state assessment design should clearly outline how tools may/may not be used and still allow unbiased evaluation of pupil ability [[58]](https://scottybreaksitdown.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/Australian-Framework-for-Generative-AI-in-Schools.pdf), [[106]](https://www.eursc.eu/BasicTexts/2025-01-D-65-en-2.pdf).

**Watch for**
- Privacy/records integrity: vetting guidance notes AI outputs may become education records; hallucinations can create incorrect records and trigger amendment processes [[54]](https://fpf.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/Ed_AI_legal_compliance.pdf_FInal_OCT24.pdf).  
- EU regulatory risk: AI used to evaluate learning outcomes and steer learning can be “high-risk” in the EU AI Act framing [[57]](https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/annex/3/), [[105]](https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/policies/regulatory-framework-ai).

---

### Category 5 — Writing, literacy, and composition support (Tier 2; central to integrity debates)
**What it looks like**
- Brainstorming; outlining; sentence-level feedback; translation; style/tone adjustment; revision coaching.

**Methods teachers use**
1) **“AI must be a tool, not the final product.”** SREB recommends AI in early stages (brainstorm/draft) with classroom time for polishing and student voice [[63]](https://www.sreb.org/sites/main/files/file-attachments/2025_ai_in_k-12classroom_guidance.pdf).  
2) **Transparency routines**: require students to annotate where AI helped, share prompt logs or conversation links, and reflect on how they evaluated/edit AI suggestions [[73]](https://www.edutopia.org/article/student-use-ai-helpful-framework/).  
3) **Fact-checking and lateral reading as default.** ODE recommends requiring students to corroborate AI-provided information with other sources and explicitly warns about fabricated sources [[65]](https://www.oregon.gov/ode/educator-resources/teachingcontent/Documents/ODE_Generative_Artificial_Intelligence_(AI)_in_K-12_Classrooms_2023.pdf).  
4) **Detectors as non-solution.** ODE warns AI detectors can be inaccurate and misidentify formal academic language [[65]](https://www.oregon.gov/ode/educator-resources/teachingcontent/Documents/ODE_Generative_Artificial_Intelligence_(AI)_in_K-12_Classrooms_2023.pdf); RAND reports many students fear being falsely accused [[110]](https://www.rand.org/content/dam/rand/pubs/research_reports/RRA4100/RRA4180-1/RAND_RRA4180-1.pdf).  

**Watch for**
- Cheating/plagiarism pressures remain central in OECD governance discussions [[4]](https://www.oecd.org/en/publications/oecd-digital-education-outlook-2023_c74f03de-en/full-report/emerging-governance-of-generative-ai-in-education_3cbd6269.html).  
- Equity and language: tools can help multilingual learners, but can also privilege “standard” dialect norms and introduce bias [[65]](https://www.oregon.gov/ode/educator-resources/teachingcontent/Documents/ODE_Generative_Artificial_Intelligence_(AI)_in_K-12_Classrooms_2023.pdf).

---

### Category 6 — Inquiry, debate, and project-based learning (PBL) (Tier 2; high upside with good design)
**What it looks like**
- AI as research starter, “counter-perspective generator,” scenario/simulation builder, project planning assistant.

**Methods**
1) **Use AI for “counter-arguments” and perspective-taking**: SREB recommends structured use to explore opposing viewpoints and strengthen argumentation [[63]](https://www.sreb.org/sites/main/files/file-attachments/2025_ai_in_k-12classroom_guidance.pdf).  
2) **Scenario-based prompts**: generate role-play contexts; then students must cite evidence and justify choices (AI provides setting, students provide reasoning) [[63]](https://www.sreb.org/sites/main/files/file-attachments/2025_ai_in_k-12classroom_guidance.pdf).  
3) **Teacher uses AI to draft a standards-aligned PBL outline**, then refines with local context and rigorous inquiry criteria [[63]](https://www.sreb.org/sites/main/files/file-attachments/2025_ai_in_k-12classroom_guidance.pdf).  

**Watch for**
- Misinformation and bias risks in research; treat AI as a starting point, not source authority [[65]](https://www.oregon.gov/ode/educator-resources/teachingcontent/Documents/ODE_Generative_Artificial_Intelligence_(AI)_in_K-12_Classrooms_2023.pdf), [[47]](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/generative-artificial-intelligence-ai-in-education).

---

### Category 7 — Creative media production (images/audio/video) (Tier 2; stronger IP/safety needs)
**What it looks like**
- Students generate images, music bases, storyboards, posters; then remix, critique, and justify design choices.

**Classroom-ready methods**
- Assign AI a bounded **role** (e.g., “design stylist,” “name generator,” “base-layer music generator”) and require human curation (“curator of AI”) [[76]](https://creativecomputing.gse.harvard.edu/genai/GenAI_StudentProjects_Dec2024.pdf).  
- Use AI for accessibility-aware design checks (fonts, readability) [[76]](https://creativecomputing.gse.harvard.edu/genai/GenAI_StudentProjects_Dec2024.pdf).  
- Combine with UDL: offer multiple forms of expression (visual, audio, written) [[14]](http://udloncampus.cast.org/wicket/resource/org.cast.cwm.xml.FileXmlDocumentSource/usr/local/tomcat/content/downloads/udlg_graphicorganizer_v2-2_numbers-no.pdf).

**Watch for**
- Copyright and cultural IP rights (explicit in Australia’s framework, including ICIP) [[58]](https://scottybreaksitdown.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/Australian-Framework-for-Generative-AI-in-Schools.pdf).  
- Deepfake and manipulation concerns in broader governance; monitoring and transparency become important [[56]](https://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/ai/NIST.AI.600-1.pdf), [[107]](https://dera.ioe.ac.uk/id/eprint/41041/1/Generative%20AI%20product%20safety%20expectations%20-%20GOV.UK.pdf).

---

### Category 8 — Coding/STEM learning supports (Tier 2; needs verification discipline)
**What it looks like**
- Explain code line-by-line; generate code snippets; debug; compare algorithms; generate spreadsheet formulas.

**Evidence-based patterns**
- Singapore MyBotBuddy explicitly supports debugging and clarifying code, but student outcomes depended on engagement patterns [[30]](https://repository.isls.org/bitstream/1/10943/1/ICLS2024_2217-2218.pdf).  
- Practical prompt patterns include “explain each line,” “why is this error happening,” “suggest two approaches and compare,” which align to computing education guidance emphasizing multiple viewpoints and explanation generation [[70]](https://computingeducationresearch.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/AICT-Guidance.pdf), [[76]](https://creativecomputing.gse.harvard.edu/genai/GenAI_StudentProjects_Dec2024.pdf).

**Watch for**
- Overtrusting code that “looks right”; require testing and explanation.  
- Model errors in math/logic: guidance cautions to check math carefully [[70]](https://computingeducationresearch.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/AICT-Guidance.pdf).

---

### Category 9 — Language learning and multilingual access (Tier 2; strong equity potential)
**What it looks like**
- Translation, interpretation, vocabulary support, rewriting for clarity; family communication in home languages.

**Methods**
- SREB explicitly suggests translating assignments/materials and “just-in-time interpretation” [[63]](https://www.sreb.org/sites/main/files/file-attachments/2025_ai_in_k-12classroom_guidance.pdf).  
- Use a verification routine: GenAI for colloquial phrasing + cross-check with another translation tool [[76]](https://creativecomputing.gse.harvard.edu/genai/GenAI_StudentProjects_Dec2024.pdf).

**Watch for**
- Privacy when translating family communications (avoid personal data in prompts) [[47]](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/generative-artificial-intelligence-ai-in-education), [[52]](https://www.education.govt.nz/school/digital-technology/generative-ai).  
- Cultural/linguistic bias (especially for underrepresented languages) [[52]](https://www.education.govt.nz/school/digital-technology/generative-ai).

---

### Category 10 — Special education / IEP drafting and sensitive supports (Tier 3)
**What it looks like**
- Drafting narrative sections; summarizing; suggesting accommodations—**high-risk** because IEPs are legally binding and contain sensitive data.

**Evidence**
- CDT reports increasing special education teacher use of GenAI in IEP/504 processes (e.g., summarizing, trend identification, accommodations), raising IDEA/FERPA concerns and the risk of insufficient individualization [[77]](https://cdt.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/2025-10-28-CDT-AI-IEP-Brief-1.pdf).  

**Implementation stance**
- If used at all: require **strict privacy controls**, strong human review, and collaborative team oversight; avoid feeding identifiable sensitive data into public models [[54]](https://fpf.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/Ed_AI_legal_compliance.pdf_FInal_OCT24.pdf), [[47]](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/generative-artificial-intelligence-ai-in-education).  

---

## 5) Risks, debates, and mitigation: what high-quality guidance converges on

### 5.1 Core risk categories (with guidance consensus)
1) **Academic integrity & authenticity**: OECD flags cheating as a first-order disruption [[4]](https://www.oecd.org/en/publications/oecd-digital-education-outlook-2023_c74f03de-en/full-report/emerging-governance-of-generative-ai-in-education_3cbd6269.html); NZ requires authenticity policies and bans GenAI in certain external assessments [[52]](https://www.education.govt.nz/school/digital-technology/generative-ai).  
2) **Privacy & data protection**: DfE and NZ advise against using personal data in GenAI tools [[47]](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/generative-artificial-intelligence-ai-in-education), [[52]](https://www.education.govt.nz/school/digital-technology/generative-ai); privacy vetting explains education-record implications and the difficulty of de-identification in practice [[54]](https://fpf.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/Ed_AI_legal_compliance.pdf_FInal_OCT24.pdf).  
3) **Hallucinations / misinformation**: DfE and NZ require checking outputs [[47]](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/generative-artificial-intelligence-ai-in-education), [[52]](https://www.education.govt.nz/school/digital-technology/generative-ai); NIST lists confabulation as a major GenAI risk [[56]](https://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/ai/NIST.AI.600-1.pdf).  
4) **Bias, discrimination, and cultural harm**: NZ highlights indigenous knowledge and language weaknesses [[52]](https://www.education.govt.nz/school/digital-technology/generative-ai); Australia’s framework centers fairness and non-discrimination [[58]](https://scottybreaksitdown.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/Australian-Framework-for-Generative-AI-in-Schools.pdf); California’s GenAI equity evaluation stresses systematic impact analysis and monitoring for vulnerable communities [[60]](https://www.genai.ca.gov/wp-content/uploads/sites/360/2024/12/GenAI-Equity-Guidelines-2024.12.19.pdf).  
5) **Safety/age-appropriateness and harmful content**: DfE says safeguarding is top priority and requires supervision and filtering/monitoring [[47]](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/generative-artificial-intelligence-ai-in-education); DfE product safety expectations specify filtering, logging, and jailbreak resistance [[107]](https://dera.ioe.ac.uk/id/eprint/41041/1/Generative%20AI%20product%20safety%20expectations%20-%20GOV.UK.pdf).  
6) **IP/copyright**: DfE and Australia require copyright compliance and warn against unpermitted use for training/fine-tuning [[47]](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/generative-artificial-intelligence-ai-in-education), [[58]](https://scottybreaksitdown.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/Australian-Framework-for-Generative-AI-in-Schools.pdf), [[107]](https://dera.ioe.ac.uk/id/eprint/41041/1/Generative%20AI%20product%20safety%20expectations%20-%20GOV.UK.pdf).  
7) **Overreliance and skill attrition**: OECD highlights skill attrition as a governance challenge [[4]](https://www.oecd.org/en/publications/oecd-digital-education-outlook-2023_c74f03de-en/full-report/emerging-governance-of-generative-ai-in-education_3cbd6269.html); surveys show teacher concern about student dependency [[88]](https://www.koreaherald.com/article/10652914).  
8) **Vendor lock-in / tool sprawl / third-party risk**: NIST emphasizes third-party integration risks and recommends procurement controls (SLAs, SBOMs, assurance reports) [[56]](https://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/ai/NIST.AI.600-1.pdf); CoSN/CGCS maturity tool embeds vendor/contract governance and monitoring [[53]](https://www.cosn.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/Cosn_CGCS-K-12-Gen-AI-Maturity-Tool-Conference.pdf).

### 5.2 Mitigation strategies that repeatedly appear (actionable patterns)
**A. Classroom-level safeguards**
- **Verification routines** (fact-check, triangulate sources, test code, check math) [[47]](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/generative-artificial-intelligence-ai-in-education), [[65]](https://www.oregon.gov/ode/educator-resources/teachingcontent/Documents/ODE_Generative_Artificial_Intelligence_(AI)_in_K-12_Classrooms_2023.pdf), [[70]](https://computingeducationresearch.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/AICT-Guidance.pdf).  
- **Transparent use norms**: annotate AI assistance, share prompt logs, reflect on accept/reject decisions [[73]](https://www.edutopia.org/article/student-use-ai-helpful-framework/).  
- **Assessment redesign**: emphasize process evidence, in-class checkpoints, local/personalized data collection, oral defense, iterative drafts—reduces the “benefit” of copying AI output [[65]](https://www.oregon.gov/ode/educator-resources/teachingcontent/Documents/ODE_Generative_Artificial_Intelligence_(AI)_in_K-12_Classrooms_2023.pdf).  
- **Explicit permissions**: “red/yellow/green” AI-use zones per task [[73]](https://www.edutopia.org/article/student-use-ai-helpful-framework/); ensure students know what is allowed.

**B. School/district governance**
- **Guidance vs policy clarity**: TeachAI distinguishes flexible guidance from binding policy and stresses stakeholder/legal review and alignment to existing integrity/privacy rules [[6]](https://www.teachai.org/toolkit).  
- **Readiness and maturity models**: CoSN/CGCS provides staged maturity levels and explicit evidence requirements (policies, training, procedures) across leadership, data, security, legal/risk, etc. [[53]](https://www.cosn.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/Cosn_CGCS-K-12-Gen-AI-Maturity-Tool-Conference.pdf).  
- **Equity evaluation before procurement**: California requires identifying impacted communities, proxy-variable bias risks, human oversight plans, transparency/notice, and continuous monitoring [[60]](https://www.genai.ca.gov/wp-content/uploads/sites/360/2024/12/GenAI-Equity-Guidelines-2024.12.19.pdf).  
- **Product safety expectations in procurement**: DfE’s expectations specify requirements for filtering, monitoring, privacy notices, DPIAs, security against jailbreaking, and IP protection [[107]](https://dera.ioe.ac.uk/id/eprint/41041/1/Generative%20AI%20product%20safety%20expectations%20-%20GOV.UK.pdf).  
- **Vendor vetting for data practices**: privacy guidance emphasizes whether student data is used to train models; how education-record rights are handled; and the risk of hallucinated education records [[54]](https://fpf.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/Ed_AI_legal_compliance.pdf_FInal_OCT24.pdf).  
- **Use NIST AI RMF structure**: govern–map–measure–manage as a lifecycle approach; NIST’s GenAI profile adds governance, content provenance, pre-deployment testing, and incident disclosure focus [[55]](https://www.nist.gov/itl/ai-risk-management-framework), [[56]](https://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/ai/NIST.AI.600-1.pdf), [[104]](https://www.nist.gov/itl/ai-risk-management-framework/nist-ai-rmf-playbook).  
- **Standardized transparency rubrics**: 1EdTech’s GenAI data rubric pushes suppliers to disclose AI use, opt-in/out, and data-source practices; can be required in RFPs [[111]](https://www.1edtech.org/standards/ai-rubric).

**C. “Do not cross” lines (common across multiple frameworks)**
- Don’t input student personal/sensitive data into public GenAI tools without strong contractual and technical controls [[47]](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/generative-artificial-intelligence-ai-in-education), [[52]](https://www.education.govt.nz/school/digital-technology/generative-ai), [[54]](https://fpf.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/Ed_AI_legal_compliance.pdf_FInal_OCT24.pdf).  
- Don’t let AI be the sole decision-maker for grading/placement/IEP content; keep humans accountable and able to override [[66]](https://www.ed.gov/sites/ed/files/documents/ai-report/ai-report-core-messages.pdf), [[106]](https://www.eursc.eu/BasicTexts/2025-01-D-65-en-2.pdf), [[58]](https://scottybreaksitdown.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/Australian-Framework-for-Generative-AI-in-Schools.pdf).  
- Don’t rely on detectors as the backbone of integrity; they are error-prone and can drive false accusations (also reflected in student fears) [[65]](https://www.oregon.gov/ode/educator-resources/teachingcontent/Documents/ODE_Generative_Artificial_Intelligence_(AI)_in_K-12_Classrooms_2023.pdf), [[110]](https://www.rand.org/content/dam/rand/pubs/research_reports/RRA4100/RRA4180-1/RAND_RRA4180-1.pdf).

---

## 6) Future trends (2026–2029) that will shape practical classroom AIGC use

### 6.1 Regulation and compliance will become more operational (not theoretical)
- **EU AI Act timelines**: prohibited practices (including emotion recognition in education) already effective Feb 2025; major transparency rules in Aug 2026; high-risk system obligations phased in Aug 2026–Aug 2027 [[105]](https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/policies/regulatory-framework-ai). This will pressure vendors and schools toward clearer documentation, logging, oversight, and disclosures.  
- **Product-level safety requirements will spread**: DfE’s detailed product safety expectations (filtering, monitoring, DPIAs, jailbreak resistance) are a template other jurisdictions may emulate [[107]](https://dera.ioe.ac.uk/id/eprint/41041/1/Generative%20AI%20product%20safety%20expectations%20-%20GOV.UK.pdf).  
- **Governance maturity will be expected**: districts will be judged by whether they have policy, evidence, monitoring, incident response, and vendor controls—not just “innovation pilots” [[53]](https://www.cosn.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/Cosn_CGCS-K-12-Gen-AI-Maturity-Tool-Conference.pdf), [[56]](https://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/ai/NIST.AI.600-1.pdf).

### 6.2 National and state “platformization” will accelerate
- Estonia’s AI Leap shows the direction: **national procurement + enterprise-grade controls + teacher training** [[80]](https://e-estonia.com/ai-leap-2025-estonia-sets-ai-standard-in-education/), [[81]](https://openai.com/index/estonia-schools-and-chatgpt/). Similar “system platforms” (state-built or centrally procured) are likely to expand because they reduce tool sprawl and improve privacy/safety enforceability.

### 6.3 AI literacy will formalize into competency frameworks and mass PD
- UNESCO has moved toward explicit AI competency frameworks for teachers and students and broader rights-based guidance [[100]](https://www.iicba.unesco.org/en/artificial-intelligence-and-education-systems), [[101]](https://www.unesco.org/sites/default/files/medias/fichiers/2024/07/UNESCO-Draft-AI-competency-frameworks-for-teachers-and-school-students_0.pdf).  
- Large-scale PD partnerships (e.g., ISTE+ASCD with Google to reach millions of educators over three years starting 2026) signal that AI literacy will become mainstream professional learning infrastructure [[115]](https://iste.org/news/iste-ascd-and-google-partner-to-provide-ai-literacy-training-to-six-million-u-s-educators).

### 6.4 Assessment redesign will move from “integrity policing” to “learning architecture”
- OECD highlights that conventional homework/assignments/exams are being challenged [[4]](https://www.oecd.org/en/publications/oecd-digital-education-outlook-2023_c74f03de-en/full-report/emerging-governance-of-generative-ai-in-education_3cbd6269.html).  
- Emerging best practice trends point toward **process-rich evidence**, oral defense, drafts with commentary, localized data collection, and explicitly permitted tool use with attribution—rather than bans or detectors [[65]](https://www.oregon.gov/ode/educator-resources/teachingcontent/Documents/ODE_Generative_Artificial_Intelligence_(AI)_in_K-12_Classrooms_2023.pdf), [[73]](https://www.edutopia.org/article/student-use-ai-helpful-framework/), [[110]](https://www.rand.org/content/dam/rand/pubs/research_reports/RRA4100/RRA4180-1/RAND_RRA4180-1.pdf).

### 6.5 Agentic and multimodal workflows will increase risk *and* potential
- Education guidance is already distinguishing agentic AI as tools that act (not just generate) [[6]](https://www.teachai.org/toolkit). As these become common, schools will need stronger identity/access controls, logging, incident response, and procurement assurance—consistent with NIST’s third-party and governance recommendations [[56]](https://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/ai/NIST.AI.600-1.pdf).

---

## 7) Recommendations for educators and school leaders (actionable, classroom-realistic)

### 7.1 Start with a “use-case portfolio” (don’t start with a tool)
Build a portfolio across the three zones:

1) **Immediate wins (Tier 1, teacher-facing)**  
   - Quiz/question generation, lesson hooks, first drafts of rubrics, substitute plans—then teacher edits [[33]](https://educationendowmentfoundation.org.uk/projects-and-evaluation/projects/choices-in-edtech-using-generative-ai-chatgpt-for-ks3-science-lesson-preparation-2024-teacher-choices-trial), [[71]](https://www.edutopia.org/article/ai-created-sub-plans-right-prompts-mean-better-outputs/), [[70]](https://computingeducationresearch.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/AICT-Guidance.pdf).  
2) **High-learning-upside pilots (Tier 2, student-facing with scaffolds)**  
   - Tutoring protocols in coding/math, writing revision coaching with transparency logs, multilingual supports with verification [[30]](https://repository.isls.org/bitstream/1/10943/1/ICLS2024_2217-2218.pdf), [[84]](https://journals.helsinki.fi/lumat/article/download/2877/2306/11219), [[73]](https://www.edutopia.org/article/student-use-ai-helpful-framework/), [[63]](https://www.sreb.org/sites/main/files/file-attachments/2025_ai_in_k-12classroom_guidance.pdf).  
3) **Restrict / slow-roll (Tier 3, high-stakes)**  
   - Grading automation, IEP full drafting, placement decisions—require formal governance, contracts, and human review; consider prohibition until controls are proven [[54]](https://fpf.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/Ed_AI_legal_compliance.pdf_FInal_OCT24.pdf), [[106]](https://www.eursc.eu/BasicTexts/2025-01-D-65-en-2.pdf), [[77]](https://cdt.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/2025-10-28-CDT-AI-IEP-Brief-1.pdf).

### 7.2 Adopt standard classroom routines that make AIGC educationally productive
- **The “Attempt–Prompt–Verify–Reflect” loop** (post it on the wall; model it weekly). Finland and Singapore evidence suggests students won’t do SRL behaviors spontaneously [[84]](https://journals.helsinki.fi/lumat/article/download/2877/2306/11219), [[30]](https://repository.isls.org/bitstream/1/10943/1/ICLS2024_2217-2218.pdf).  
- **Process evidence as assessment**: require drafts, annotations of AI influence, and reflection; this aligns to Edutopia-style transparency routines and reduces integrity problems [[73]](https://www.edutopia.org/article/student-use-ai-helpful-framework/), [[65]](https://www.oregon.gov/ode/educator-resources/teachingcontent/Documents/ODE_Generative_Artificial_Intelligence_(AI)_in_K-12_Classrooms_2023.pdf).  
- **Teach verification explicitly**: lateral reading, checking sources, testing code; ODE explicitly recommends corroborating AI claims with other sources [[65]](https://www.oregon.gov/ode/educator-resources/teachingcontent/Documents/ODE_Generative_Artificial_Intelligence_(AI)_in_K-12_Classrooms_2023.pdf).  
- **Use UDL intentionally**: generate multiple representations and expression options, not just “easier worksheets” [[14]](http://udloncampus.cast.org/wicket/resource/org.cast.cwm.xml.FileXmlDocumentSource/usr/local/tomcat/content/downloads/udlg_graphicorganizer_v2-2_numbers-no.pdf).

### 7.3 Put procurement and privacy on rails (so teachers aren’t forced to improvise)
- Use a **district maturity model** (CoSN/CGCS) to define what “ready” means: leadership, data governance, security, legal/risk, monitoring, vendor contracts [[53]](https://www.cosn.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/Cosn_CGCS-K-12-Gen-AI-Maturity-Tool-Conference.pdf).  
- Require **supplier transparency** (1EdTech rubric; DfE product safety expectations where applicable) [[111]](https://www.1edtech.org/standards/ai-rubric), [[107]](https://dera.ioe.ac.uk/id/eprint/41041/1/Generative%20AI%20product%20safety%20expectations%20-%20GOV.UK.pdf).  
- Implement a **privacy vetting checklist** aligned to FPF guidance: what counts as PII, whether prompts train models, how records/explanations/amendments work, and whether hallucinations could create incorrect student records [[54]](https://fpf.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/Ed_AI_legal_compliance.pdf_FInal_OCT24.pdf).  
- Prefer **institutional accounts and controlled environments** over personal accounts, to reduce data leakage and inequitable access (a known pattern in teacher surveys) [[39]](https://www.turing.ac.uk/sites/default/files/2025-06/understanding_the_impacts_of_generative_ai_use_on_children_-_wp1_report.pdf).

### 7.4 Build a coherent AI literacy progression (students and staff)
- Align to UNESCO’s competency direction and your local digital citizenship standards; emphasize: bias, privacy, verification, promptcraft, attribution, and “when not to use AI” [[100]](https://www.iicba.unesco.org/en/artificial-intelligence-and-education-systems), [[101]](https://www.unesco.org/sites/default/files/medias/fichiers/2024/07/UNESCO-Draft-AI-competency-frameworks-for-teachers-and-school-students_0.pdf), [[58]](https://scottybreaksitdown.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/Australian-Framework-for-Generative-AI-in-Schools.pdf).  
- Close the training gap: RAND finds student training is scarce and many students report teachers haven’t taught AI use explicitly [[110]](https://www.rand.org/content/dam/rand/pubs/research_reports/RRA4100/RRA4180-1/RAND_RRA4180-1.pdf). Make AI literacy explicit in curriculum maps, not optional assemblies.

### 7.5 Measure what matters (and avoid “vanity metrics”)
- For teacher-facing tools: track **workload time**, **resource quality**, and **teacher confidence** (EEF model) [[33]](https://educationendowmentfoundation.org.uk/projects-and-evaluation/projects/choices-in-edtech-using-generative-ai-chatgpt-for-ks3-science-lesson-preparation-2024-teacher-choices-trial), [[34]](https://d2tic4wvo1iusb.cloudfront.net/production/documents/projects/chatgpt_in_lesson_planning_-_evaluation_report.pdf).  
- For student-facing tools: track **learning outcomes + SRL behaviors** (prompt logs, revision quality), not just “engagement” [[30]](https://repository.isls.org/bitstream/1/10943/1/ICLS2024_2217-2218.pdf), [[84]](https://journals.helsinki.fi/lumat/article/download/2877/2306/11219).  
- For equity: perform **pre-procurement equity reviews** and ongoing monitoring (California model) [[60]](https://www.genai.ca.gov/wp-content/uploads/sites/360/2024/12/GenAI-Equity-Guidelines-2024.12.19.pdf).

---

## 8) Open problems and research gaps (what’s still unresolved)
- High-quality K–12 intervention research remains limited relative to the speed of adoption; systematic reviews call for more longitudinal and experimental studies on learning outcomes and conditions of effectiveness [[78]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2025.1647573/full).  
- The field is still negotiating the “right” balance between **innovation** and **rights-based safeguards** (privacy, equity, contestability), which UNESCO and other bodies highlight as central [[17]](https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/id/eprint/10176438/), [[100]](https://www.iicba.unesco.org/en/artificial-intelligence-and-education-systems).  
- Governance is shifting from “Should we allow ChatGPT?” to “What is our controlled ecosystem, assessment model, and accountability system?”—a move already visible in Estonia’s national program design and in product safety expectations frameworks [[80]](https://e-estonia.com/ai-leap-2025-estonia-sets-ai-standard-in-education/), [[107]](https://dera.ioe.ac.uk/id/eprint/41041/1/Generative%20AI%20product%20safety%20expectations%20-%20GOV.UK.pdf).

---

### Bottom line (practical synthesis)
The strongest global pattern is: **AIGC delivers reliable value first as a teacher productivity and design tool** (with rigorous evidence of workload reduction without quality loss in England) [[33]](https://educationendowmentfoundation.org.uk/projects-and-evaluation/projects/choices-in-edtech-using-generative-ai-chatgpt-for-ks3-science-lesson-preparation-2024-teacher-choices-trial), [[34]](https://d2tic4wvo1iusb.cloudfront.net/production/documents/projects/chatgpt_in_lesson_planning_-_evaluation_report.pdf), while **student-facing impact is highly contingent on scaffolding, transparency, verification routines, and self-regulation supports** (Singapore, Finland, Michigan) [[30]](https://repository.isls.org/bitstream/1/10943/1/ICLS2024_2217-2218.pdf), [[84]](https://journals.helsinki.fi/lumat/article/download/2877/2306/11219), [[37]](https://michiganvirtual.org/blog/have-you-considered-ai-in-your-classroom-a-khanmigo-pilot-story/). Systems moving fastest and safest are building **centralized governance + vetted tools + professional learning + clear assessment rules**, rather than leaving adoption to informal personal accounts and uneven classroom norms (Estonia, NSW; and governance toolkits like CoSN/CGCS, DfE expectations, NIST) [[80]](https://e-estonia.com/ai-leap-2025-estonia-sets-ai-standard-in-education/), [[31]](https://www.digital.nsw.gov.au/strategy/case-studies/nsweduchat), [[53]](https://www.cosn.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/Cosn_CGCS-K-12-Gen-AI-Maturity-Tool-Conference.pdf), [[107]](https://dera.ioe.ac.uk/id/eprint/41041/1/Generative%20AI%20product%20safety%20expectations%20-%20GOV.UK.pdf), [[56]](https://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/ai/NIST.AI.600-1.pdf).
